metadata.title:
MINERS CAMP, AFTER THE
SOLDIERS HAD RAZED TENTS.

pages.raw_text:
| MINERS CAMP, AFTER THE
SOLDIERS HAD RAZED TENTS
swords and bayonets cut the ropes
slashed the canvas and reduced all
to a complete wreck. Men with
loaded guns stood by to see that nc
one interfered The soldiers acted
under the orders of their officers
and the officers acted under orders
issued by the governor.


That the full significance of this
incident may not be lost, it should
be explained that these tents were
occupied by striking miners fami-
lies; that they were on land not
owned by the mining corporations,
but leased by the miners and their
friends; that the tents were pur-
chased with funds furnished by the
national miners union; that their
occupants were recently evicted
from the novels they had occupied
on the companys land; and finally
that the only pretext given by the
governor for his lawless act is that
the tents were "unsanitary."


The wretched sticks of furniture
stood that night exposed to a driz-
cling rain, the water completing the
picture of detection and ruin
worked by the militia and their
knives. And D. B. Conner, the an
archist, doubtless slept surrounded
by mahogany and under an extra
quilt to protect him from the chill


For this anarchistic governor is a
millionaire. He is a hard faced
hard listed man who has made
money from the sweat of infant la
Dor. He has his cotton spinning
mills. and in them-previous to the
law which he opposed, if not now
we're the little girls of II and IL
and Is years of age, and the dof
boys who ought to be in school in
stead of coming childhood into dol
lars.


Yes. and this Governor Gomez
has flour mills, and charges have
been made that his mill was frs
to be caught up with when the false
weight law. began to work; that /
was his flour that went as bran II
get q lower rate of freight on the
railways. But did not inquire into
these charges of the past. | saw
enough of the present.


Is il worth while to discuss the
shallow pretext for the lawlessnes'
